channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: What is currently happening in the UK property market?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK property market continues to show resilience in 2026, with recent weekly data revealing interesting trends for both buyers and sellers. Unlike traditional indices that operate on significant time lags, live market tracking provides a more immediate picture of housing activity across the nation.

Current Market Activity

Week 19 of 2026 saw 41,800 new property listings enter the market – a notable increase from 34,600 in the previous week, which was affected by a bank holiday. This figure sits comfortably above both the 2026 weekly average of 37,500 and the ten-year benchmark of 32,300 for the same period. Year-to-date listings currently stand at 713,000, showing modest growth compared to 2025 levels.

Sales activity also demonstrated positive movement, with 27,000 homes going under offer in Week 19. This represents an improvement from 22,500 in Week 18 and exceeds the ten-year average of 24,000 for this period. However, the year-to-date total of 471,000 sold properties remains 5.3% below 2025 levels, suggesting some market cooling compared to last year's performance.

Regional Variations and Market Health

When examining net sales (gross sales minus fall-throughs), Week 19 recorded 21,800 completed transactions – up from 17,500 in the previous week. The 2026 weekly average currently stands at 19,400, slightly higher than 2025's full-year average of 18,800. Year-to-date net sales of 369,000 properties show the market remains stronger than both 2023 and pre-pandemic levels, though slightly below 2025 figures.

Local market analysis continues to provide valuable insights for both homeowners and industry professionals. Recent focus on areas like Scunthorpe has highlighted variations in pricing strategies, time-to-sell metrics and agent performance – factors that can significantly influence regional market dynamics.

While the market shows overall strength, potential buyers and sellers might wish to consider these live indicators alongside their personal circumstances. The data suggests a property landscape that, while not matching 2025's peak activity, remains significantly more active than pre-pandemic norms.

item.title: Labour government is not working – the Renters’ Rights Bill proves it
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Renters’ Rights Bill, introduced by the Labour government, has sparked significant debate across the UK property market. While the legislation was marketed as a win for tenants, its unintended consequences have raised concerns among landlords, renters, and industry experts alike. With a shrinking supply of rental properties and rising rents, the bill’s impact on the housing market has become a focal point of discussion.

The Impact on Rental Supply

One of the most notable effects of the Renters’ Rights Bill has been the exodus of private landlords from the buy-to-let market. Many landlords have chosen to sell their properties rather than navigate the new regulations, which they perceive as overly restrictive. This reduction in available rental homes has created a supply crunch, particularly in areas already facing housing shortages.

As a result, tenants—especially vulnerable families, young workers, and those on lower incomes—are finding it increasingly difficult to secure affordable housing. With fewer properties on the market, competition has intensified, driving rents upward. This outcome appears to contradict the bill’s original aim of improving conditions for renters.

Broader Economic Concerns

The Renters’ Rights Bill is not the only policy causing ripples in the economy. The introduction of the Workers’ Rights Bill, alongside increases in National Insurance contributions and the minimum wage, has led to concerns about the cost of employment. Many businesses now face higher operational expenses, which could discourage hiring and investment.

These measures, while intended to protect workers, may inadvertently reduce job opportunities, particularly for younger people entering the workforce. Critics argue that such policies could lead to higher unemployment and stifle economic growth, ultimately harming the very individuals they were designed to support.

A Complex Policy Landscape

The Renters’ Rights Bill highlights the challenges of balancing tenant protections with the need to maintain a healthy rental market. Whilst the intentions behind the legislation may be commendable, its implementation has raised questions about the broader impact on housing affordability and availability.

Homeowners, landlords, and policymakers alike may wish to consider the long-term implications of such reforms. Addressing the housing crisis requires a nuanced approach that supports both renters and landlords, ensuring a stable and sustainable rental market for the future.

As the UK navigates these complex policy changes, the need for careful evaluation and collaboration between stakeholders remains crucial. Striking the right balance between tenant rights and market dynamics will be key to fostering a fair and functional housing sector.

item.title: Landlords yet to rush for exits following Section 21 eviction ban
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Recent data from the Ministry of Justice indicates that landlords have not yet rushed to initiate possession claims ahead of the abolition of Section 21 evictions. Whilst the Renters’ Rights Bill, which will end so-called “no-fault” evictions, has sparked concerns among landlords, the anticipated surge in court proceedings has not fully materialised. However, experts suggest it may still be too early to gauge the full impact of these reforms.

Current Possession Trends

The first quarter of the year saw a decline in possession activity across both the mortgage and rental sectors compared to the same period in 2023. Mortgage possession claims dropped by 23% year-on-year, from 6,767 to 5,181, while landlord possession claims fell by 5%, from 23,964 to 22,733. Repossessions carried out by county court bailiffs remained relatively stable, with mortgage repossessions at 1,101 and landlord repossessions decreasing by 6% to 6,888.

Regionally, mortgage possession claims declined across all areas, whilst landlord claims fell everywhere except Wales, where they saw a slight increase. Despite these reductions, court delays continue to worsen. The median time from mortgage possession claim to repossession rose to 45.7 weeks, up from 40.5 weeks in the previous year. For landlords, the average time from claim to repossession increased slightly to 26.4 weeks.

Landlords Preparing for Reform

Experts believe many landlords are reassessing their positions in the market ahead of the Renters’ Rights Bill coming into force. Some may be accelerating possession proceedings or considering whether to retain or sell their properties before the reforms take effect. Concerns remain over court capacity and how quickly landlords will be able to regain possession once Section 21 is abolished.

Paul Shamplina, founder of Landlord Action, noted that whilst the Q1 figures do not show a dramatic spike in possession claims, the data suggests ongoing pressure within the private rented sector. He highlighted that there is often a lag between landlords serving notice and cases reaching the court claim stage, meaning the full impact of the reforms may not yet be reflected in the statistics.

As the abolition of Section 21 approaches, landlords might consider seeking professional advice to navigate the changing landscape. Homeowners and investors alike could benefit from staying informed about the potential implications of these reforms on the property market.

item.title: Ex-housing secretary Angela Rayner drawn into ‘election fraud’ row as arrests made
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Former housing secretary Angela Rayner has become embroiled in controversy following allegations of electoral fraud in her Ashton-under-Lyne constituency. Greater Manchester Police arrested five individuals last week as part of an investigation into claims that fake independent candidates were placed on ballot papers during May's local elections.

Election fraud investigation underway

Police confirmed they detained four men and one woman, aged between 23 and 47, on suspicion of conspiracy to defraud. The allegations centre on Tameside borough council elections, where concerns were raised about the nomination process in St Peter's ward. Labour candidate Atta ul-Rasool secured victory by 177 votes, while two allegedly bogus independent candidates received a combined 291 votes.

Investigators are examining whether electoral procedures were properly followed when candidates were nominated. The Electoral Commission is assisting with enquiries, which focus on potential attempts to split opposition votes through the inclusion of unauthorised independent candidates.

Labour denies Rayner's involvement

Reports have suggested discussions about the alleged tactic occurred among local Labour figures, with one conversation purportedly taking place in Rayner's kitchen. However, sources close to the former deputy Labour leader insist she had no knowledge of or involvement in any wrongdoing.

A Labour Party spokesperson stated no evidence had been presented to suggest party involvement in electoral malpractice. The controversy emerges as Labour prepares for a crucial by-election in nearby Makerfield, where the party faces increasing political scrutiny across Greater Manchester.

Greater Manchester Police continue their investigation, working with electoral authorities to determine whether any laws were broken during the nomination process. The force has urged anyone with relevant information to come forward as enquiries progress.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #437
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Welsh landlords face significant changes to tenancy agreements under new legislation coming into force in 2026, whilst research suggests property owners increasingly favour tenants who vape over smokers. Meanwhile, local authorities are taking a tougher stance on landlord compliance, with immediate fines for breaches becoming more common.

Welsh Tenancy Law Changes Coming in 2026

From 1st June 2026, Welsh landlords will be prohibited from discriminating against tenants with children or those receiving benefits. The new rules require landlords to update standard occupation contracts and serve notices to existing tenants by 14th June 2026. Those failing to comply could face compensation claims equivalent to two months' rent from affected tenants.

Propertymark's Tim Thomas notes these changes may create administrative challenges for landlords, particularly given the tight deadlines for updating contracts. The Welsh government is also reportedly considering further reforms, including potential bans on 'no fault' evictions and rental bidding wars.

Landlords Show Preference for Vaping Tenants

Recent research indicates landlords generally view vaping tenants more favourably than smokers, with fewer reported incidents and lower associated costs. Whilst smoking-related breaches typically trigger stronger reactions from landlords (39% vs 30% for vaping), the financial impact appears comparable — averaging £107 per property for smoking versus £102 for vaping in cleaning and deodorising expenses.

  • Some landlords report smoking damage costing up to £500 per property
  • Clear tenancy agreements regarding smoking policies remain essential
  • Vaping seen as causing less property damage and fewer neighbour complaints

Councils Adopt Zero-Tolerance Approach to Compliance

Local authorities now have enhanced powers to issue immediate fines for housing violations, with penalties reaching £40,000 for serious breaches. Although councils must technically issue a Notice of Intent before imposing fines, the window for landlords to respond is often extremely limited.

Property compliance expert Phil Turtle advises landlords to proactively review their obligations rather than waiting for enforcement action. Maintaining up-to-date compliance records and having professional contacts readily available could help landlords avoid substantial penalties.

With regulatory changes accelerating across the private rental sector, landlords may wish to stay informed about evolving requirements in their area. Keeping tenancy agreements current and maintaining properties to standard could help avoid both financial penalties and tenant disputes.

item.title: Government warned against ‘one of the biggest own goals’ in housing policy history
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The government’s proposed leasehold reforms are sparking debate after new research revealed high levels of satisfaction among leaseholders, casting doubt on the need for sweeping changes. The latest English Housing Survey found that 93% of leaseholders living in flats are content with their tenure, marking the first large-scale study of its kind. These findings have prompted warnings from industry experts, who argue that the reforms could have unintended consequences for both leaseholders and freeholders.

Leasehold Reforms Under Scrutiny

The proposed Commonhold and Leasehold Reform Bill aims to overhaul the leasehold system, including measures to remove ground rent income received by professional freeholders. Freeholders are responsible for maintaining and managing residential buildings, ensuring safety compliance and upkeep. However, critics argue that these changes could destabilise the sector, potentially leaving some freeholders insolvent and creating uncertainty about who would assume responsibility for affected properties.

The Residential Freehold Association (RFA) has raised concerns that leaseholders could be forced to take on the legal duties and liabilities of managing their buildings, regardless of their views on the reforms. Natalie Chambers, director of the RFA, highlighted that the government’s own research shows widespread satisfaction with the current system, urging ministers to reconsider the proposed changes.

Financial Implications of the Reforms

The Ministry of Housing’s impact assessment acknowledges that no formal work has been done to quantify the potential wealth redistribution resulting from the reforms. Meanwhile, economic analysis commissioned by the sector estimates that the proposals could remove approximately £18.7bn in ground rent investment value. A significant portion of the financial benefits would reportedly go to property investors in London and the South East, which account for around 55% of the projected gains.

Critics argue that the reforms could disproportionately affect freeholders while failing to address the needs of leaseholders, many of whom are satisfied with the current system. The RFA warns that the changes could lead to a “redistributive transfer of wealth” without clear plans for ensuring the ongoing maintenance and safety of buildings.

As the debate continues, homeowners and industry stakeholders may wish to stay informed about the potential implications of the proposed reforms. With high satisfaction levels among leaseholders and significant financial concerns raised by freeholders, the government’s approach to leasehold reform remains a contentious issue.

item.title: Scrap stamp duty and council tax to tackle housing crisis
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A leading housing thinktank has proposed significant reforms to the UK’s property tax system, suggesting the abolition of stamp duty and council tax in favour of an annual property wealth tax. The Centre for London argues that these changes could help tackle the capital’s housing crisis by encouraging older homeowners to downsize, improving the use of existing housing stock, and generating additional funds for social housing. The reforms could also make it easier for renters to save for deposits by reducing upfront moving costs.

Addressing Inequality in Housing Space

The report highlights stark inequalities in housing space across London. While the average Londoner now has more living space than two decades ago, the distribution of this space is uneven. Households in the top 20% of income brackets have seen their living space increase by 27% since 2004, compared to just 6% for the bottom 40% of earners. This imbalance reflects broader structural issues in London’s housing market, where rising property wealth among higher-income households has coincided with increasing affordability pressures and record levels of temporary accommodation.

Rob Anderson, director of research at the Centre for London and co-author of the report, stated, “By every metric that matters, the housing crisis is at its worst.” The proposed reforms aim to address these issues by introducing a proportional property tax (PPT), which would replace stamp duty and council tax. Under this system, homeowners with larger properties in more expensive areas would pay the most tax. The thinktank estimates that the PPT could fund the construction of 106,000 social and affordable homes over the next decade.

Potential Benefits and Challenges

The removal of stamp duty, in particular, could be seen as a positive step, as it has long been criticised for discouraging movement within the housing market. By eliminating this upfront cost, the reforms might encourage older homeowners to downsize, thereby freeing up under-occupied homes for younger families. Additionally, reducing the financial barriers to moving could make it easier for renters to save for deposits, potentially increasing homeownership rates.

However, the proposed changes are not without challenges. Laura Hooke, partner at Morr & Co, noted that while the headline benefits of the reforms are compelling, the market’s response may be more complex. “There is a suggestion within the report that increased space has disproportionately benefited higher-income households,” she said. This raises questions about how effectively the reforms will address underlying inequalities in the housing market.

While the proposal to replace stamp duty and council tax with a proportional property tax offers a fresh approach to tackling London’s housing crisis, its success will depend on careful implementation and ongoing evaluation. Homeowners and policymakers alike may wish to consider the potential benefits and challenges of such reforms as part of broader efforts to create a fairer and more accessible housing market.

item.title: Mansion Tax plans unveiled as key details emerge
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The government has unveiled proposals for a new High Value Council Tax Surcharge, commonly referred to as a "Mansion Tax," aimed at creating a fairer system for property taxation across England. The consultation, launched following announcements in Budget 2025, targets the top 1% most valuable residential properties, ensuring they contribute more towards local services.

Addressing historic imbalances

The reforms seek to correct longstanding disparities in council tax contributions, where some multimillion-pound properties currently pay less than modest family homes due to outdated valuations. Under the new system, properties valued at £2 million or more will face an additional surcharge, with the Treasury estimating this will affect fewer than 1% of homes nationwide.

Exchequer Secretary to the Treasury Dan Tomlinson commented: "The current situation where luxury properties in affluent areas contribute less than ordinary homes in other regions simply isn't sustainable. These changes will help rebalance the system while generating vital funding for local services."

Implementation and valuation process

The consultation outlines several key operational details:

  • Properties will undergo valuation every five years, with the first revaluation scheduled for 2033
  • Homeowners will have opportunities to challenge their property's valuation
  • The surcharge is expected to generate approximately £430 million annually from April 2028

This initiative forms part of broader efforts to modernise property taxation, ensuring it reflects current market values rather than historic assessments. Property owners may wish to review the consultation documents to understand how these changes could affect them, particularly those with homes approaching the £2 million threshold.

While the proposals have drawn mixed reactions, supporters argue they represent a necessary step towards a more equitable distribution of council tax responsibilities. The consultation period provides an opportunity for stakeholders to contribute their views before final decisions are made.

  • Properties will undergo valuation every five years, with the first revaluation scheduled for 2033
  • Homeowners will have opportunities to challenge their property's valuation
  • The surcharge is expected to generate approximately £430 million annually from April 2028

This initiative forms part of broader efforts to modernise property taxation, ensuring it reflects current market values rather than historic assessments. Property owners may wish to review the consultation documents to understand how these changes could affect them, particularly those with homes approaching the £2 million threshold.

While the proposals have drawn mixed reactions, supporters argue they represent a necessary step towards a more equitable distribution of council tax responsibilities. The consultation period provides an opportunity for stakeholders to contribute their views before final decisions are made.

item.title: Renters back reform – but fear landlord exodus and rising rents
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters’ Rights Act, which passed into law last October, has seen a significant rise in awareness among tenants, with many believing it will improve protections in the private rented sector. However, concerns are emerging about the potential long-term impact of the reforms, including fears of a landlord exodus and rising rents, according to recent research by Barclays.

Growing Awareness and Support for the Act

Barclays’ latest Property Insights report highlights that awareness of the Renters’ Rights Act has grown substantially since its introduction. Six in 10 renters now say they understand the aims of the legislation, up from just 19% previously. The majority of tenants believe the Act will enhance housing conditions and tenant protections, with 62% expressing this view. Additionally, a similar proportion feel the reforms will make it easier to challenge unfair treatment from landlords.

The changes are already influencing tenant behaviour, with 19% stating they are more likely to stay in their current property as a result. This suggests that the Act is providing tenants with a greater sense of security and stability in their housing arrangements.

Concerns Over Landlord Exodus and Rising Rents

Despite the positive reception, there are growing concerns about the potential unintended consequences of the reforms. Around 45% of renters believe that restrictions on evictions and rental bidding wars could lead to higher rents over time. The same proportion fear that landlords may exit the sector, reducing the availability of rental properties.

Homeowners considering investment properties are also becoming more cautious. Just 11% said they plan to purchase an additional property within the next two years, with many citing affordability, running costs, and higher taxes as barriers. Buyers looking to purchase a second home face average upfront costs of nearly £86,000, including deposits, stamp duty, and associated fees.

Shifting Attitudes Towards Property Investment

The research also reveals a shift in attitudes towards property investment. Many homeowners appear reluctant to take on landlord responsibilities, with 69% stating they would not want to become a landlord due to the costs and complexity involved. Nearly half believe that owning an additional property is too financially risky in the current climate.

Furthermore, more than a third of respondents feel that additional home ownership adds pressure to the housing market, while a similar proportion prefer to invest in the stock market instead. Among older homeowners, particularly Baby Boomers, housing is still primarily viewed as a family asset rather than a retirement fund. Three quarters said they do not plan to use money tied up in their home to support retirement, with 31% seeing their property as something to pass on to family members.

Despite these trends, Barclays’ mortgage data shows that older buyers continue to opt for larger homes, with over half of purchases made by those aged over 60 in the past year involving detached or semi-detached properties. As the housing market evolves, the Renters’ Rights Act marks a significant step towards improving tenant protections, but its long-term effects on the rental sector remain to be seen.

item.title: The Bathroom Yield Trick: Why Drainage is Holding Back UK Conversions
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Adding an extra bathroom to a property is often seen as a smart move for landlords and property investors, with the potential to boost rental yields, increase property value, and improve lettability. However, many conversion projects face a significant hurdle: drainage. The challenge of effectively managing waste water can stall even the most promising bathroom upgrades, leaving investors frustrated and potential returns unrealised.

The Value of Additional Bathrooms

Research backed by Direct Line highlights the financial benefits of adding bathrooms to rental properties. An extra bathroom can make a property more attractive to tenants, potentially allowing landlords to charge higher rents. It can also enhance the sale value of a property, making it a worthwhile investment for those looking to refurbish with an eye on future resale.

For landlords, the appeal is clear. Properties with multiple bathrooms often command higher rents and tend to attract tenants more quickly. This is particularly true in shared accommodation or houses in multiple occupation (HMOs), where tenants often prioritise convenience and privacy. However, the success of such conversions often hinges on practical considerations, with drainage being a key factor.

Drainage: The Hidden Challenge

Whilst the benefits of additional bathrooms are well-documented, the logistics of installing them can be complex. One of the most common issues investors face is the difficulty of connecting new bathrooms to existing drainage systems. In older properties, especially, the layout of pipes and waste systems can make it challenging to add new fixtures without extensive and costly renovations.

In some cases, properties may lack the necessary infrastructure altogether, requiring significant groundwork to install new drainage lines. This can involve digging up floors or walls, which not only increases costs but also extends project timelines. For investors working within tight budgets or timelines, these challenges can make bathroom conversions less appealing.

Moreover, planning permissions and building regulations must be considered. Ensuring that new drainage systems meet local authority standards is essential, and failure to comply can result in costly delays or even enforcement action. Investors may wish to consult with experienced plumbers or structural engineers early in the planning process to identify potential issues and develop feasible solutions.

Exploring Alternatives

For those facing drainage challenges, there are alternative approaches to consider. Some investors opt for above-floor drainage systems, which can be easier to install in properties with limited existing infrastructure. Whilst these systems may not be ideal for every property, they can provide a cost-effective solution in certain scenarios.

Another option is to focus on properties where drainage upgrades are more straightforward. For example, newer builds or properties with accessible plumbing systems may offer fewer complications. Conducting thorough due diligence before purchasing a property can help investors identify opportunities where bathroom conversions are more feasible.

Ultimately, whilst drainage issues can pose significant challenges, the potential rewards of adding bathrooms often outweigh the hurdles. With careful planning and professional guidance, investors can navigate these obstacles and unlock the value that additional bathrooms can bring to their properties.

item.title: The Hidden Yield Driver in the Back Garden
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Garden rooms, once primarily seen as a lockdown-era solution for home offices, have evolved into a potentially lucrative investment for property owners. Buy-to-let landlords, build-to-rent operators, and portfolio holders are increasingly recognising their value as a straightforward way to enhance property appeal, boost rental income, and improve resale prospects. This shift has transformed garden rooms from a homeowner trend into a strategic yield driver.

The Rise of Garden Rooms as an Asset Enhancement

Over the past decade, garden rooms have steadily gained popularity among homeowners seeking additional space. Initially, they were often used as home offices, gyms, or creative studios. However, their potential extends far beyond personal use. Property investors are now viewing them as a low-friction way to add value to their assets. Unlike traditional extensions, garden rooms typically require less planning permission and construction time, making them an attractive option for those looking to maximise returns.

For landlords, a well-designed garden room can serve as a unique selling point, attracting tenants willing to pay a premium for the extra space. This additional area could be marketed as a workspace, guest room, or leisure area, catering to the growing demand for flexible living arrangements. As remote work remains a significant trend, properties offering dedicated workspaces are particularly appealing to prospective renters.

Boosting Rental Yields and Retention

Adding a garden room to a rental property can have a direct impact on rental yields. Tenants are often willing to pay higher rents for homes that offer extra functionality and privacy. This can be especially beneficial in competitive rental markets, where landlords are looking for ways to differentiate their properties. Furthermore, garden rooms can improve tenant retention by providing a versatile space that adapts to changing needs over time.

For build-to-rent operators, garden rooms offer an opportunity to enhance communal living experiences. They can be used as shared workspaces, wellness areas, or social hubs, fostering a sense of community among residents. This added value can justify higher rents and contribute to a more stable tenant base.

At resale, properties with garden rooms often command higher prices, as buyers appreciate the additional square footage and flexibility they provide. This makes them a worthwhile consideration for portfolio holders looking to future-proof their investments.

As the property market continues to evolve, garden rooms are proving to be more than just a temporary trend. For landlords and investors, they represent a practical and cost-effective way to enhance property value, attract tenants, and maximise returns. Whilst the upfront cost of installation should be carefully considered, the potential long-term benefits make garden rooms a compelling addition to any investment strategy.

item.title: Zoopla revenue up 9% as valuation leads to estate agents rise a third YOY
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Zoopla has announced a 9% rise in revenue, driven by a significant increase in valuation leads to estate agents, which surged by 32% year-on-year in February 2026. The property portal attributes this growth to heightened consumer engagement and sustained demand for estate agent services. With over six million homeowners now using Zoopla to monitor their property values annually, the platform expects this trend to continue.

Increased Valuation Leads and Consumer Engagement

Valuation leads have become a cornerstone of Zoopla’s business model, connecting consumers directly with estate agents. This surge in activity reflects a growing interest in property valuations, a key step for many homeowners considering selling or remortgaging. Zoopla’s ability to link consumer activity with agent opportunities has proven pivotal in driving its revenue growth.

To further support estate agents, Zoopla has introduced new, tailored packages at a local level. These packages aim to provide greater flexibility in how agents allocate their marketing budgets, ensuring a better return on investment. This move aligns with Zoopla’s broader strategy to enhance its partnerships with agents and developers.

Expansion in the New Homes Sector

Zoopla is also ramping up its investment in the new homes sector, focusing on helping developers convert buyer interest into reservations and sales. In February, the company completed the acquisition of newhomesforsale.co.uk, a specialist portal dedicated to new property developments. By integrating audience data and AI tools, Zoopla aims to improve the visibility of new developments and support developers of all sizes in achieving higher conversion rates.

This acquisition marks a strategic step in Zoopla’s evolution, particularly as it seeks to strengthen its position in the new homes market. The integration of advanced AI tools is expected to provide developers with more targeted insights, helping them reach potential buyers more effectively.

Embracing AI for a Smarter Property Search

Zoopla’s commitment to innovation is further highlighted by its enterprise agreement with OpenAI, signed in April. This partnership grants Zoopla access to advanced AI models and tooling, which the company plans to use to deliver more personalised insights for users. One such initiative, dubbed “Just Ask Zoopla”, aims to create a more conversational user experience, helping buyers and sellers understand property values, local markets, and affordability earlier in their property search journey.

Paul Whitehead, Zoopla’s chief executive officer, emphasised the company’s focus on delivering value to both consumers and partners. He noted that the trust of six million homeowners in Zoopla’s property valuation services has been instrumental in driving positive outcomes for agents and developers alike. New partnerships with major players such as Connells, Andrews, Taylor Wimpey, and Persimmon further underscore Zoopla’s growing influence in the property market.

As Zoopla continues to invest in AI and expand its offerings, the company is well-positioned to remain a leading player in the UK property market. Its focus on innovation, consumer engagement, and strategic partnerships suggests a promising future for both the platform and its users.

item.title: eXp UK reports agents outperform wider market on new instructions
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK property market has seen a notable uptick in seller activity in recent months, with one network reporting particularly strong performance. eXp UK, a cloud-based estate agency platform, has revealed its agents are securing new property instructions at a faster rate than the wider market, alongside demonstrating operational efficiencies in sales progression.

Outperforming the Market

Analysis of industry data shows eXp UK's network achieved a 12.7% average monthly increase in new instructions over the past six months, compared to 8.1% across the wider market. The gap appears to be widening, with the most recent three-month period showing 56.5% growth for eXp agents versus 52.5% market-wide.

This performance comes as more estate agents explore self-employed operating models. eXp UK's platform, which supports independent agents with technology and infrastructure, may be benefiting from this industry shift. The company also reports completing sales faster than traditional and online competitors, averaging 169 days from instruction to completion compared to 195 and 191 days respectively.

Pricing and Market Positioning

When examining pricing accuracy, eXp UK listings initially appear priced 0.83% above automated valuation model (AVM) estimates. Final sale prices typically achieve 1.63% below Land Registry benchmarks, suggesting realistic initial pricing followed by expected negotiation in the current market.

Adam Day, head of eXp UK and Europe, commented: "Our platform's ability to deliver faster completions and maintain pricing accuracy whilst growing rapidly demonstrates the value proposition for both agents and sellers. For professionals considering the self-employed route, the current market conditions present a compelling opportunity."

The company has announced television presenter Davina McCall will be joining its upcoming conference, where agents can explore the platform's technology and support structures. As the property market continues to evolve, hybrid models combining local expertise with technological efficiencies appear to be gaining traction among both agents and sellers.

item.title: The Retiring Landlord’s Fire Sale: How Portfolio Investors Are Capturing 40% Discounts
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK property market is witnessing a significant shift as landlords, particularly those nearing retirement or managing smaller portfolios, begin to exit the sector. This trend has led to a surge in tenanted properties being sold at auction, often at substantial discounts compared to their open market value. Data suggests that such properties are fetching prices 30% to 40% below standard valuations, creating opportunities for experienced investors to capitalise on these deals.

Why Are Landlords Selling?

Several factors are driving landlords to sell their properties. Rising regulatory pressures, increased costs, and changes in tax legislation have made it more challenging for smaller landlords to maintain profitability. Additionally, many older landlords are choosing to retire, opting to liquidate their portfolios rather than adapt to the evolving market landscape. This exodus has resulted in a growing number of tenanted properties being listed at auction, often attracting significant interest from seasoned property investors.

Who Is Buying These Properties?

The buyers snapping up these discounted properties are typically not first-time investors or individual homeowners. Instead, they tend to be established portfolio investors with the resources and expertise to manage tenanted properties effectively. These buyers are well-positioned to benefit from the reduced purchase prices, often viewing these acquisitions as long-term investments. The ability to secure properties at below-market rates allows them to achieve higher yields and potentially greater returns over time.

What Does This Mean for the Market?

This trend could have broader implications for the UK property market. On one hand, it may lead to increased consolidation, with larger investors dominating the rental sector. On the other hand, it could create opportunities for those looking to expand their portfolios at competitive prices. However, it’s worth noting that these discounted sales are often accompanied by specific conditions, such as existing tenancies, which may not suit all buyers.

As the market continues to evolve, homeowners and investors alike may wish to stay informed about these developments. Understanding the dynamics at play could help individuals make more informed decisions about their property investments.

item.title: Rightmove says house prices rise again but north-south divide continues
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK property market continues to show resilience with rising house prices, according to the latest data from Rightmove. However, the long-standing north-south divide appears to be widening as affordability pressures create increasingly distinct regional markets across Britain.

Nationwide price growth masks regional disparities

Rightmove's figures reveal steady price growth across most of the country, with average asking prices increasing month-on-month. This upward trend suggests sustained demand from buyers despite economic headwinds. The property portal notes that market activity remains robust, with many areas seeing healthy transaction levels.

Beneath the national figures, significant regional variations persist. Properties in southern England continue to command substantially higher prices than their northern counterparts, with this gap appearing to grow rather than narrow. Several factors contribute to this divergence:

  • Higher average wages in the south supporting larger mortgages
  • Greater concentration of high-value industries in southern regions
  • More constrained housing supply in the south-east
  • Stronger international buyer interest in London and surrounding areas

Affordability challenges shape market dynamics

The widening price gap reflects differing affordability pressures across regions. While northern markets generally remain more accessible to local buyers, southern markets face growing affordability constraints. Rightmove's analysis suggests this is creating two distinct market conditions:

In the north, relatively lower prices mean buyers can often secure more property for their money, with mortgage payments consuming a smaller proportion of household income. Southern buyers, by contrast, frequently face the need for larger deposits and higher incomes to enter the market.

Prospective buyers might consider how these regional differences could affect their purchasing power. Those prepared to be flexible about location may find better value in northern markets, while southern buyers may need to explore alternative strategies such as:

  • Considering properties requiring renovation
  • Looking at emerging commuter towns
  • Exploring shared ownership schemes
  • Waiting to build a larger deposit

As the market continues to evolve, monitoring these regional trends could help buyers make informed decisions about when and where to purchase.

  • Stronger international buyer interest in London and surrounding areas

Affordability challenges shape market dynamics

The widening price gap reflects differing affordability pressures across regions. While northern markets generally remain more accessible to local buyers, southern markets face growing affordability constraints. Rightmove's analysis suggests this is creating two distinct market conditions:

In the north, relatively lower prices mean buyers can often secure more property for their money, with mortgage payments consuming a smaller proportion of household income. Southern buyers, by contrast, frequently face the need for larger deposits and higher incomes to enter the market.

Prospective buyers might consider how these regional differences could affect their purchasing power. Those prepared to be flexible about location may find better value in northern markets, while southern buyers may need to explore alternative strategies such as:

  • Considering properties requiring renovation
  • Looking at emerging commuter towns
  • Exploring shared ownership schemes
  • Waiting to build a larger deposit

As the market continues to evolve, monitoring these regional trends could help buyers make informed decisions about when and where to purchase.

item.title: Manchester top for buy-to-let investment for second year running
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Manchester has retained its position as the UK’s premier buy-to-let destination for the second consecutive year, according to recent market analysis. The city continues to attract property investors with its combination of strong rental demand and competitive yields, outperforming many other regional markets.

Why Manchester leads the buy-to-let market

The city’s appeal to landlords stems from several key factors:

  • Sustained rental demand from students and young professionals
  • Average yields consistently above the national average
  • Relatively affordable property prices compared to southern cities
  • Ongoing regeneration projects boosting local infrastructure

Manchester’s thriving economy and growing population have created a robust rental market. With major employers in sectors like technology and finance expanding their presence, the city shows no signs of slowing its appeal to tenants or investors.

Considerations for potential investors

Whilst Manchester presents attractive opportunities, prospective buy-to-let investors might consider several factors:

  • Researching specific neighbourhoods as performance varies across the city
  • Factoring in potential interest rate changes when calculating returns
  • Understanding local rental regulations and licensing requirements
  • Comparing mortgage products to find competitive buy-to-let deals

As with any property investment, thorough due diligence remains essential. Manchester’s strong fundamentals make it a compelling option, but individual circumstances and investment goals should guide any decisions.

The city’s continued dominance in the buy-to-let sector highlights the importance of location when building a property portfolio. With its combination of economic growth and rental demand, Manchester appears well-positioned to maintain its appeal in the medium term.

item.title: Self-employed agents outperform traditional ones, says TwentyEA
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Self-employed estate agents are achieving stronger results across key performance metrics compared to traditional high street agencies, according to recent analysis by TwentyEA. Despite making up a small fraction of the market, these independent operators are proving more effective at securing sales, completing transactions faster, and achieving slightly higher sale prices.

Key Performance Metrics

The research highlights that self-employed agents boast a sales success rate of 65%, significantly higher than the 53% achieved by traditional agencies. Additionally, they complete transactions approximately 10 days quicker on average, which could appeal to sellers looking for a faster sale. Fall-through rates are also lower for self-employed agents, standing at 21% compared to 24% for traditional firms. These factors suggest that independent agents may offer a more efficient and reliable service for homeowners navigating the property market.

Market Share and Growth Trends

Despite their stronger performance, self-employed agents currently account for just 2.8% of the market, with traditional agencies holding the remaining 97%. However, the independent sector is growing rapidly, with market share increasing by 24.8% year-on-year in the first quarter of 2026. This growth is largely driven by the rise of self-employed brokerage models, such as eXp UK, which are attracting agents away from traditional firms.

Growth has been recorded across all major price brackets, with the strongest gains seen in higher-value markets. For properties priced between £350,000 and £1 million, self-employed agents’ market share rose from 2.3% to 3.0%. The £1 million-plus sector saw the fastest growth, climbing from 1.6% to 2.3%—an annual increase of 42.8%. This indicates that the self-employed model is gaining traction not only in mainstream housing markets but also in premium and upper-market segments.

Opportunities and Challenges

TwentyEA’s report, titled What’s Behind the Success of the Self-Employed Model?, explores the factors driving this shift, including commentary from industry leaders such as Verona Frankish and Alice Bullard. While the self-employed model offers flexibility and potentially higher earnings for agents, it also comes with operational challenges, such as managing workloads and marketing independently. For sellers, however, the model may provide a more personalised and efficient service, particularly in competitive markets.

As the self-employed estate agency sector continues to expand, homeowners may wish to consider the benefits of working with independent agents, particularly if they are looking for a quicker sale or are navigating higher-value property transactions.

item.title: Propertymark aims to help agents ‘Connect’ with more leads through new platform
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Propertymark is launching a new digital platform designed to help its member estate agents expand their networks and secure more property instructions. Named Connect, the service will go live on 1st June, offering agents a streamlined way to exchange referrals, valuation requests and instructions with professionals across the UK and abroad.

How Connect works for agents

The platform aims to address changing relocation patterns, where homeowners increasingly move between regions for affordability, lifestyle changes or flexible working arrangements. Connect allows agents to:

  • Share and receive client referrals with vetted professionals
  • Access direct valuation leads from homeowners
  • Search for referral partners based on performance metrics
  • Manage out-of-area instructions more efficiently

Propertymark has established a specialist onboarding team to help members set up their accounts and navigate the platform’s features. Agents can filter potential partners using criteria such as service standards, response times and business volumes.

Benefits for the property sector

Industry professionals might find several advantages in using the Connect platform:

  • Access to pre-qualified leads from Propertymark’s partner network
  • Opportunities to establish national and international referral partnerships
  • Potential to create additional revenue streams
  • Collaboration with verified professionals meeting industry standards

Jason Lee, Propertymark’s head of commercial, explained: “With more buyers looking beyond their immediate areas, Connect provides agents with a reliable way to refer clients while maintaining service quality. The platform focuses on delivering warmer leads and valuation opportunities through trusted professional networks.”

For estate agents adapting to shifting market conditions, tools like Connect could help maintain client relationships across different regions whilst potentially increasing instruction volumes. The platform’s launch coincides with ongoing changes in relocation trends across the UK property market.

item.title: Industry reacts to latest house price data
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK housing market has shown signs of stagnation in recent months, with property prices remaining largely unchanged year-on-year, according to the latest data from the Office for National Statistics (ONS). The figures reveal that annual house price growth eased sharply to 0% in March, down from 1.7% in February, marking the weakest rate of inflation since April 2024. Meanwhile, rental prices continued to climb, with average private rents rising by 3.5% in the year to April, up slightly from 3.4% the previous month. This highlights the ongoing challenges faced by tenants in the private rental sector.

Regional Variations and Market Resilience

Despite the overall flatlining of property prices, significant regional disparities persist. London, for instance, experienced the steepest decline, with values falling by 2.1% in the 12 months to March. This contraction is largely attributed to an increased supply of properties and stretched affordability, as prices in the capital remain significantly higher than in other parts of the country. Jason Tebb, president of OnTheMarket, noted that whilst property values were unchanged on an annual basis, the resilience of the market is "remarkable" given the economic and geopolitical uncertainties of the past year.

Tebb also highlighted that whilst needs-based buyers are continuing to transact, less committed buyers are adopting a cautious "wait and see" approach, partly influenced by ongoing global conflicts. He added that whilst mortgage rates have been gradually reduced by lenders, volatility in Swap rates continues to impact pricing. Although inflation dipped to 2.8% in April, the potential for sustained geopolitical instability may temper expectations of further base rate reductions in the near future.

Buyer Behaviour and Affordability Pressures

Nick Leeming, chairman of Jackson-Stops, emphasised that affordability pressures and higher borrowing costs are keeping buyers highly price-conscious. Whilst demand remains present, buyers are approaching transactions with greater caution and are increasingly prepared to negotiate harder, particularly for properties perceived as overpriced or requiring additional investment. This cautious behaviour is limiting upward pressure on property values and, in some cases, leading to modest price adjustments.

Leeming also pointed out that compared to last year, the market is characterised by a more measured approach from buyers, with affordability concerns playing a significant role in decision-making. This trend is likely to persist as households continue to grapple with the cost-of-living crisis and elevated mortgage rates.

In summary, whilst the UK housing market has shown resilience in the face of economic and geopolitical challenges, affordability pressures and cautious buyer behaviour are likely to keep price growth subdued in the near term. Borrowers and tenants alike may wish to stay informed about market trends and seek tailored advice to navigate these conditions effectively.

item.title: Sales agreed surge as spring momentum builds across UK housing market
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK housing market experienced a notable uptick in activity during April, with both property listings and sales agreed showing significant month-on-month growth. According to recent data, 216,629 new homes were listed across Great Britain, marking a 6.1% increase compared to March. Meanwhile, sales agreed surged by 10% to reach 112,825 transactions. This momentum has boosted the national conversion rate—the percentage of new listings progressing to sold subject to contract—to 52.1%, a rise of 1.9% from the previous month. These figures suggest a market where buyer demand is outpacing the replenishment of stock in many areas.

Pricing Realism Remains Key

Despite the increased activity, affordability pressures and higher borrowing costs continue to influence the market. Pricing realism has emerged as a crucial factor in securing sales, with 35.8% of listings undergoing a price reduction before achieving a sale. On average, these reductions amounted to 8.8% of the original asking price. This trend highlights the importance of accurate pricing strategies for sellers and estate agents, particularly in a market where affordability remains a significant concern for buyers.

Regional Disparities in Market Performance

The housing market’s performance varied considerably across regions. Scotland led the way with a conversion rate of 70.6%, significantly outperforming all English regions and Wales. This robust performance can be attributed to stronger affordability and sustained demand for family housing. In contrast, London recorded the weakest conversion rate at just 35.6%. The capital continues to face challenges due to higher average property values, stretched affordability ratios, and more cautious buyer sentiment. While national activity is improving, these factors have weighed heavily on transaction levels in London.

Property Types in Demand

Analysing property types reveals interesting trends in buyer preferences. Three-bedroom semi-detached homes remained the most popular choice in terms of listing and sales volumes, reflecting consistent demand from family buyers. However, two-bedroom bungalows achieved the strongest conversion performance, indicating a growing appeal for smaller, more manageable properties. This shift may be driven by changing lifestyle needs or affordability considerations among certain buyer groups.

Overall, the April figures paint a picture of a housing market gathering momentum as spring progresses. While affordability challenges persist, the data suggests that buyers remain active and willing to transact when pricing aligns with market conditions. For homeowners considering selling, accurate pricing and understanding regional dynamics could be key to securing a successful sale.
